content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N, followed by N lines, each containing two integers x and y representing the coordinates of a point.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of nice quadrangles that can be formed using the given points.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The two nice quadrangles are formed by the points (1, 1), (2, -1), (-1, -1), (-3, -4) and (1, 1), (2, 2), (-1, -1), (-2, 2).
There are no nice quadrangles that can be formed using the given points.
There are eight nice quadrangles that can be formed using the given points.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to iterate over all possible combinations of four points and check if they form a nice quadrangle. A nice quadrangle is one where the points A, B, C, and D satisfy the given conditions and the area of the quadrangle is an integer. We can calculate the area using the shoelace formula and check if it is an integer by verifying if the remainder when divided by 2 is 0.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^4) per test case, O(T * N^4) total. The time complexity is high due to the four nested loops.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space complexity is constant as we only use a fixed amount of space to store the points and the count of nice quadrangles.
